Description: Pre-allocate Vec capacity when the size is known or can be estimated to avoid repeated reallocations and copies
Do: Use Vec::with_capacity(n) when you know or can estimate the number of elements
Dont: Use Vec::new() and push in a loop without pre-allocating when the approximate size is known
Measurement: cargo bench; compare allocs with DHAT or heaptrack

Issue: Avoid String cloning in hot paths
Description: Cloning Strings allocates new heap memory and copies bytes. Use references or Cow<str> to avoid unnecessary allocations
Do: Pass &str references when ownership is not needed; use Cow<str> when you sometimes need owned data; use String::from only when ownership is required
Dont: Clone strings in loops or pass owned Strings when a reference would suffice
Measurement: cargo bench --bench allocation; compare with DHAT profiler

Description: SmallVec stores up to N elements inline on the stack before falling back to heap allocation, avoiding allocation for small collections
Do: Use SmallVec<[T; N]> for collections that are usually small (< 8 elements); tune N based on profiling
Dont: Use SmallVec everywhere without profiling; set N too large wasting stack space
Measurement: cargo bench; compare B/op with and without SmallVec

Description: Reuse allocated buffers by calling .clear() instead of creating new ones in loops to avoid repeated allocation and deallocation
Do: Call buffer.clear() and reuse the allocation; use std::mem::take for ownership transfer while preserving capacity
Dont: Create a new String or Vec on every loop iteration
Measurement: cargo bench; compare allocs/op with DHAT profiler

Keywords: zero copy, borrow, lifetime, parse, slice
Description: Parse data by returning references (&str, &[u8]) into the original buffer instead of allocating new owned copies
Do: Return &str slices into the original data; use nom or winnow for zero-copy parsing; carry lifetimes through the parse tree
Dont: Parse by converting every token into an owned String
Measurement: cargo bench; compare bytes allocated and throughput MB/s

Keywords: Box, heap, stack overflow, large struct
Description: Move large types to the heap with Box to avoid stack overflow and excessive stack copying
Do: Box large structs (> 1KB) and arrays; pass by reference when possible; use Box in recursive types
Dont: Allocate small structs on the heap unnecessarily; box every single type
Measurement: Check stack usage; run with RUST_MIN_STACK=8388608

Description: Order struct fields from largest to smallest alignment to minimize padding and improve cache performance
Do: Use #[repr(C)] when layout matters; order fields by decreasing alignment; use arrays of structs (AoS) vs struct of arrays (SoA) based on access patterns
Dont: Randomly order struct fields; use pointer-heavy data structures for large collections
Measurement: perf stat -e cache-misses; cargo bench with criterion

Description: Blocking operations in async tasks starve other tasks by holding the worker thread. Offload blocking work to a dedicated thread pool
Do: Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking for blocking I/O, CPU-heavy work, or calls to synchronous libraries
Dont: Call synchronous std::fs functions, blocking HTTP clients, or CPU-heavy code directly in async
Code Bad: async fn read_file(path: &str) -> std::io::Result<String> {\n    std::fs::read_to_string(path) // blocks async worker!\n}
Measurement: tokio-console; monitor task poll times and worker utilization

Issue: Use write! instead of format! for appending
Description: format! creates a new String each time. Use write! to append directly to an existing String or buffer without extra allocation
Do: Use std::fmt::Write with write! to append to String; use std::io::Write for byte buffers
Dont: Use format! and push_str repeatedly when write! would avoid intermediate allocations
Measurement: cargo bench; compare allocs/op

Description: Intern frequently repeated strings to store each unique string once and use lightweight handles for comparison
Do: Use the lasso or string-interner crate for repeated strings; use &'static str for compile-time known strings
Dont: Create owned copies of the same string thousands of times
Measurement: Memory profiling with DHAT; compare heap usage

Description: Reading or writing small amounts directly to files triggers a syscall per operation. Buffered IO batches into larger chunks
Do: Wrap File with BufReader for reading and BufWriter for writing; call flush() before dropping BufWriter
Dont: Read or write files without buffering causing excessive system calls
Measurement: strace -c to count syscalls; cargo bench for throughput

Description: Creating a new database connection per request is expensive. Connection pools maintain reusable connections
Do: Use deadpool, bb8, or r2d2 for connection pooling; configure pool size based on workload
Dont: Create a new connection per request; set pool size to max without considering database limits
Measurement: Monitor connection count; measure p99 latency

Description: Serde can deserialize &str directly from the input buffer without copying when using #[serde(borrow)]
Do: Use #[serde(borrow)] with &str fields; use serde_json::from_slice for zero-copy JSON parsing
Dont: Always deserialize into owned Strings when borrowed references would suffice
Measurement: cargo bench; compare throughput and B/op

Issue: Use a faster hasher for HashMap
Description: The default SipHash is DoS-resistant but slow. Use FxHashMap or AHashMap for internal-only maps
Do: Use FxHashMap or AHashMap for internal data structures; keep SipHash for user-controlled keys (DoS resistance)
Dont: Replace SipHash in maps keyed by user input; use slow hashers for purely internal key types
Measurement: cargo bench; compare ops/sec with criterion

Description: Use PGO and LTO to let the compiler optimize based on real workload data and cross-crate information
Do: Enable LTO in release profile; use PGO for latency-sensitive services; strip symbols for smaller binaries
Dont: Ship debug builds; skip LTO for large production binaries
Code Good:
# Cargo.toml
[profile.release]
lto = true
codegen-units = 1
strip = true
panic = "abort"
opt-level = 3

# PGO workflow:
# 1. Build instrumented: RUSTFLAGS="-Cprofile-generate=/tmp/pgo" cargo build --release
# 2. Run workload: ./target/release/app
# 3. Merge: llvm-profdata merge -o /tmp/pgo/merged.profdata /tmp/pgo
# 4. Build optimized: RUSTFLAGS="-Cprofile-use=/tmp/pgo/merged.profdata" cargo build --release
Code Bad:
# Cargo.toml
[profile.release]
# defaults: lto = false, codegen-units = 16
# misses cross-crate optimizations
# larger binary with debug symbols
Measurement: cargo build --timings; hyperfine for latency comparison

Keywords: compile time, incremental, mold, cranelift, sccache
Description: Long compile times hurt iteration speed. Use linker optimizations and caching to speed up development
Do: Use mold linker for fast dev builds; enable sccache for CI; use cargo-nextest for parallel testing
Dont: Wait for slow builds without investigating; use LTO in development profile
Code Good:
# .cargo/config.toml
[target.x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu]
linker = "clang"
rustflags = ["-C", "link-arg=-fuse-ld=mold"]

# Install sccache for caching:
# cargo install sccache
# export RUSTC_WRAPPER=sccache

# Use cranelift backend for faster dev builds:
# CARGO_PROFILE_DEV_CODEGEN_BACKEND=cranelift cargo build
Code Bad:
# Default linker (ld) is slow
# No caching: every CI build recompiles everything
# LTO in dev profile: minutes of linking
[profile.dev]
lto = true # terrible idea for dev builds
Measurement: cargo build --timings; time cargo build

Description: Calling .collect() materializes an iterator into a Vec consuming memory. Process items lazily when possible
Do: Chain iterator adapters without collecting intermediate results; use .for_each() for side effects; collect only at the end
Dont: Collect into intermediate Vecs between processing steps
Code Good:
let total: f64 = records
    .iter()
    .filter(|r| r.active)
    .map(|r| r.amount)
    .sum(); // no intermediate allocation
Measurement: cargo bench; compare with DHAT for allocation tracking

Description: Each Arc::clone() performs an atomic increment which has CPU cache coherence overhead under contention
Do: Clone Arc only when transferring ownership to a new task or thread; pass &T references within the same task
Dont: Clone Arc for every function call within the same task
Measurement: cargo bench; profile with perf to see atomic contention

Description: CPU profiling identifies which functions consume the most time. Flamegraphs visualize the call stack distribution
Do: Use cargo-flamegraph for quick CPU profiling; use perf on Linux; use Instruments on macOS
Dont: Optimize code without profiling first; guess which functions are slow
Code Good:
# Install:
# cargo install flamegraph

# Profile:
# cargo flamegraph -- --release

# Or with specific binary:
# cargo flamegraph --bin myapp -- --arg1 val1

# On Linux with perf:
# perf record -g --call-graph dwarf ./target/release/app
# perf script | inferno-flamegraph > flamegraph.svg
Measurement: cargo flamegraph; perf stat -e instructions,cycles,cache-misses

Description: DHAT tracks heap allocations to identify which code paths allocate the most memory, enabling targeted optimization
Do: Use dhat crate for Rust-native heap profiling; analyze allocation sites and total bytes allocated
Dont: Ignore allocation profiles when debugging high memory usage or slow performance due to allocator pressure
Measurement: cargo run --features dhat-heap; analyze with dh_view.html
